region_of_origin: United States (Starbucks origin); widely consumed globally including Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for green tea frappuccino; closest defensible match is blended beverage food product. Green tea / matcha specific term not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code for blended iced green tea frappuccino with milk and whipped cream. Approximate match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for green tea frappuccino as a specific food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one medium cup of approximately 350 ml, equivalent to roughly 350 g, as commonly served at cafe chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Green Tea Frappuccino — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Green Tea Frappuccino (cr_id D08010041), confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sweetened milk-based blended tea beverage
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for green tea frappuccino in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified low per University of Sydney convention) based on the closest analogous food: sweetened milk-based tea beverage with added sugar syrup. Matcha itself has negligible GI impact; the dominant GI driver is the sugar syrup and milk lactose component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 6.91 g/100g = 24.2 g) / 100 = 13.3. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar syrup with zero dietary fibre and low potassium content
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request the green tea frappuccino without sugar syrup and without whipped cream, as the added sugar (2.7 g/100 g) combined with negligible fibre (0 g/100 g) and low potassium triggers the sweetened flavoured beverage concern; sodium is low at 25 mg/100 g and does not independently elevate risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened blended beverage with added sugar syrup causing rapid glycaemic excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid the standard green tea frappuccino as it is a sugar-sweetened blended beverage (SSB) with added sugar syrup; per ADA guidelines SSBs are contraindicated regardless of the absolute sugar figure, and the estimated GI of 55 with a glycaemic load of 13.3 per medium serving represents a clinically significant glucose challenge — an unsweetened matcha with plain low-fat milk and no whipped cream is a suitable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from whipped cream and milk combined with added sugar syrup
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request the green tea frappuccino without whipped cream and without sugar syrup to reduce saturated fat (estimated 1.42 g/100 g) and added sugar intake; the EGCG catechins in matcha have demonstrated endothelial and anti-inflammatory benefits that are partially offset by the saturated fat and sugar load in the standard preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-concentration EGCG catechins from matcha with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the matcha EGCG catechin content (potent polyphenol anti-inflammatory agent) and the calcium (56 mg/100 g) supporting bone health critical for those on corticosteroids; however, whipped cream and sugar syrup should be omitted to minimise pro-inflammatory saturated fat and sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Tea Frappuccino is a blended iced cafe beverage made from matcha (powdered green tea), milk, ice, and sugar syrup, topped with whipped cream. It is widely available at international cafe chains such as Starbucks and is popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The drink has a smooth, creamy, and mildly sweet flavour with the characteristic vegetal and slightly bitter notes of matcha balanced by the sweetness of sugar syrup and the richness of whipped cream. Per 100 g, it provides 54 kcal, 1.69 g protein, 2.29 g fat, 6.91 g carbohydrate, 2.71 g sugar, 0 g dietary fibre, 25 mg sodium, and 56 mg calcium. The calcium content reflects the milk component. Dietary fibre is zero as this is a liquid blended beverage with no whole-food fibre sources. The matcha component provides EGCG catechins, which are among the most potent polyphenol anti-inflammatory agents studied in clinical nutrition. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened blended beverage (SSB) due to the added sugar syrup, making it unsuitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes per ADA guidelines. The estimated glycaemic index is 55 (low by University of Sydney convention) with a glycaemic load of approximately 13.3 per medium 350 g serving. Patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request the drink without sugar syrup and without whipped cream to reduce added sugar and saturated fat intake. An unsweetened matcha with plain low-fat milk is a clinically acceptable alternative for most chronic disease populations.

What is the GI of green tea frappuccino?
Is green tea frappuccino high in sodium?
Does green tea frappuccino have caffeine?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010041) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore foods. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for similar milk-and-cream blended beverage as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this single field is medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence) as no direct measurement exists. Local-language names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for a food widely consumed in Singapore.
notes: 1. Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (0.0 g/100 g) per HPB source — this is expected for a blended liquid beverage with no whole-food fibre sources; confirmed zero per HPB FoodData entry. 2. Saturated fat (1.42 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for a comparable milk-and-cream blended beverage (USDA FDC analogous entry for Starbucks Green Tea Frappuccino Blended Beverage) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement when available. 3. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this blended beverage preparation not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central at the level of specificity required. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk-based tea beverage from the University of Sydney GI Database; GI 55 is classified as LOW per University of Sydney convention (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); gi_category corrected to 'low' accordingly. No direct GI measurement for green tea frappuccino exists in the approved GI database as of 2026. 5. Although this beverage is of US commercial origin (Starbucks), it is widely consumed in Singapore and listed in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Local-language names in Chinese (绿茶星冰乐 / 綠茶星冰樂), Malay (Minuman teh hijau campuran berais), Tamil (பச்சை தேயிலை ஃப்ராப்புச்சினோ), and Hindi (हरी चाय फ्रैपुचीनो) are provided to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Singapore-context foods. 6. Halal status is assumed true for standard milk-based preparation without alcohol; individual outlet verification is recommended.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Added local_names for chinese_simplified ('绿茶星冰乐'), chinese_traditional ('綠茶星冰樂'), malay ('Minuman teh hijau campuran berais'), tamil ('பச்சை தேயிலை ஃப்ராப்புச்சினோ'), hindi ('हरी चाय फ्रैपुचीनो') and romanized_local to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for a food listed in the Singapore HPB database.
Iteration 3: Added all local-language name strings verbatim to the flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language to satisfy Q8 aliases consistency rule.
Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 is classified as LOW per University of Sydney convention (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), resolving Q4 failure from iteration 2.
Iteration 3: Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to clarify the University of Sydney GI classification convention.
Iteration 3: Updated notes to document gi_category correction rationale and multilingual name additions.
Iteration 3: Updated confidence_reason to reflect multilingual name additions.
